plain_rule: A written Arabic word may carry a prefixed particle (wāw, fāʾ, bāʾ, kāf, lām, or the article al-); its contribution is part of the meaning and must be shown, not hidden inside the host word.
trigger_condition: the token begins with a legal proclitic sequence
typical_error_feature: the whole token read as one stem
point_template: Look again at the start of this word: it may begin with a small attached particle.
teach_template: When a word starts with wāw/fāʾ/bāʾ/kāf/lām or al-, that piece is a separate particle with its own meaning; read the host without it, then add the particle's contribution.
bottom_out_template: Split the word into the prefixed particle and the host, and gloss each piece.

plain_rule: A pronoun attached to the end of a word (a possessive on a noun, an object on a verb) is a separate piece of meaning; a single-letter ending can also be a root letter, so without context it stays a candidate.
trigger_condition: the token ends in a possible enclitic pronoun
expected_feature: the attached pronoun surfaced, or the token kept pending
typical_error_feature: the ending folded into the stem
point_template: Look at the ending of this word: it may be an attached pronoun rather than part of the root.
teach_template: An attached pronoun at the end carries 'his/her/their/you/us'; but a lone single letter could also be a radical, so confirm from context before deciding.
bottom_out_template: Separate the enclitic pronoun from the stem and name its reference.

plain_rule: Without short vowels, the same letters can be more than one word; keep every reading as a candidate and do not pick one until evidence appears.
trigger_condition: an unvoweled token with more than one reading
expected_feature: the competing readings kept and ranked
point_template: These letters can be read more than one way; notice that the short vowels are missing.
teach_template: Unvoweled Arabic underdetermines the word: the same letters may be a noun or a verb. Keep the readings side by side and let context or a source decide.
bottom_out_template: List the competing readings with their ranking; do not assert one.

plain_rule: Some particles (mā, wāw, lā, in, ...) have several functions; the function is decided by context, not by the surface form alone.
trigger_condition: the token is or contains a multi-function particle
expected_feature: the particle's function decided from context
point_template: This little word has several possible jobs here; look at what surrounds it.
teach_template: A particle like mā or wāw can be negation, a relative, a question, coordination, and more; identify the function from the clause, then gloss it accordingly.
bottom_out_template: Name the particle's function in this clause and its effect.

plain_rule: A prefixed preposition (bāʾ, lām, kāf) forms a jar-majrūr with the following word; the relation must be shown, not the host alone.
point_template: There is a prefixed preposition here; it links to the word it governs.
teach_template: A prefixed preposition makes the following noun genitive (majrūr); read them as a phrase and show the relation.
bottom_out_template: Name the preposition and the genitive noun it governs.

arabic_grammar_name: case / mood from context (al-iʿrāb bi-ḥasab al-siyāq)
plain_rule: The case or mood ending depends on the governing element; when the ending is not visible, the reading stays a candidate until context confirms it.
trigger_condition: case/mood is not decidable from the surface
typical_error_feature: a case asserted with no visible ending
point_template: Look at the ending here and at the word that governs it before deciding the case or mood.
teach_template: A case or mood ending is assigned by a governing element; name that governor and the case it requires, and if it is not visible, keep the reading pending.
bottom_out_template: State the governor and the case/mood it assigns.

arabic_grammar_name: the governing element (al-ʿāmil) justifies the iʿrāb
plain_rule: A correct ending is not enough: the governing element must justify it. A right ending with an absent or wrong governor is unsafe reasoning and goes to review.
trigger_condition: a case is asserted without a justified governor
expected_feature: the case justified by a named governor
typical_error_feature: right answer, wrong or absent reason
point_template: Name the element that governs this word before you justify its ending.
teach_template: The ending must be justified by the ʿāmil (the governing element); naming the right case with the wrong or no governor is unsafe and is routed to review, not accepted.
bottom_out_template: Identify the governor, then justify the assigned case/mood from it.

plain_rule: An orthographic variant (hamza seat, alif form, tāʾ marbūṭa, alif maqṣūra) is normalized for matching only; the original spelling is preserved and may itself be significant.
typical_error_feature: the variant treated as an error
point_template: Notice the spelling of this letter; it is a variant that is normalized only for matching.
teach_template: Hamza seats and alif/tāʾ forms vary in writing; the original spelling is kept, and only a match key is normalized.
bottom_out_template: Show the original spelling and the normalized match key side by side.
